title: Hidden destructive account deletion vs delete account vs destructive action confirmation vs typed confirmation vs data export vs privacy settings
summary: Flag hidden destructive account deletion when account closure or associated data erasure is concealed, disguised, obstructed, or committed without account-level review; use delete account for the corrected account closure workflow, destructive action confirmation for one object delete, typed confirmation for exact target verification, data export for downloading a copy, and privacy settings for active-account controls.
decisionRules: Choose hidden destructive account deletion when users can create an account but cannot find a direct account deletion or erasure request path from account settings, app settings, or the required web resource.

Choose hidden destructive account deletion when Deactivate, Disable, Sign out, Remove from device, Download data, Unsubscribe, or Email support is presented as account deletion while the account record or associated data remains active.

Choose hidden destructive account deletion when deletion is blocked after uninstall, buried in help search, hidden behind vague support contact, lacks request status, or commits account-level destruction without naming account identity, retained data, billing, linked apps, and recovery limits.

Choose delete account when the corrected task is a discoverable account closure workflow with identity review, affected services, associated data, retained data, export-before-delete, verification, request status, and recovery or cancellation window.

Choose destructive action confirmation when the target is one file, project, key, subscription, record, workspace object, or service item rather than the user's account identity and personal-data lifecycle.

Choose typed confirmation as an added gate when users must reproduce the account email, workspace, or target phrase inside a deletion flow; typed confirmation does not solve hidden discoverability or missing data-scope review.
Choose data export when users need to download a copy before deletion or while keeping the account; export does not close the account or erase source data.
Choose privacy settings when users manage active-account privacy controls, consent, app access, data sharing, or account visibility rather than closing the account.

Do not count a deletion claim as complete when the visible path relies on deactivation-only copy, export confusion, support email with no status, hidden account identity, no uninstall-safe route, or no retained-data disclosure.
failureModes: Delete account is missing from account settings and exists only in an FAQ or support search result.
The product offers Deactivate account but keeps profile, messages, tokens, and app data active.
Download my data is placed where account deletion belongs, so users believe export deletes the account.
After uninstall, users must reinstall the app to request deletion because no web deletion resource exists.
A small settings-row Delete control commits account closure without naming account identity, affected data, retained data, billing, linked apps, or recovery limits.
Support-required deletion has no case ID, expected timing, status page, or proof that the request was received.

sources.note: Supports easy-to-find account deletion initiation, associated data deletion, direct web completion, user information, and billing handling.
Supports in-app and web deletion paths, associated data deletion, user access after uninstall, retained-data expectations, and public disclosure.
Supports review and download before delete, exact account identity, account versus service deletion distinction, and possible recovery window.
Supports erasure request timing, non-absolute erasure, recipient notification, backup handling, and legal exceptions.
Supports separating export from deletion because creating an archive is not account closure or erasure.
